title: Fukuro toji — Japanese pouch binding
subtitle: Each page a folded sheet, sewn through the open edge
excerpt: Fukuro toji, the Japanese pouch binding, uses single sheets folded in half with the fold at the foredge. The open edge is sewn through, creating a hidden pocket inside each page. It is a first bookbinding project for anyone: the sewing is a simple four-hole stab pattern, no adhesive touches the text block, and the finished book lies flat.
sourceNotes: Fukuro toji is documented in the Japanese bookbinding tradition. Structural description draws on the yotsume (four-hole stab) pattern as a related public-domain canonical structure, cross-referenced with Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), Internet Archive. The specific pouch-page construction is synthesised from general bookbinding practice. Note: Kojiro Ikegami, Japanese Bookbinding (1986, Weatherhill) is still in copyright; structural description only is used here.

glossaryTerms.definition: The Japanese pouch binding. Each page is a single sheet folded in half, with the fold at the foredge and the two open edges sewn through. The fold creates a hidden pocket that was traditionally used to conceal the blank reverse of single-sided printed sheets.
The outer edge of a book's pages, opposite the spine. In a standard binding the foredge is trimmed; in fukuro toji it carries the closed fold of each page.

The four-hole Japanese stab-binding pattern. Four holes are pierced at even intervals near the spine edge; the thread follows a specific over-and-under route that produces a clean parallel pattern on the spine face.

Any binding method where the needle passes straight through the full depth of the page stack from front cover to back cover, rather than through individual folded signatures. Japanese stab-binding traditions include yotsume, asanoha (hemp-leaf), and kikko (tortoiseshell) patterns.

content.text: Fukuro toji
is one of the oldest surviving Japanese bookbinding structures. Each page is a single sheet of paper folded in half, with the fold placed at the
foredge
and the two open edges held together at the spine by
stab binding
. The fold creates a sealed pocket along the outer edge of each page. The
yotsume

four-hole stab pattern shown here is the simplest of the Japanese stab patterns and a first bookbinding project. The finished book lies flat when open and the spine stitches become a visible part of the design.

items.name: Text paper, 70–90 gsm, cut to page size
items.qty: 1 sheet per page (each sheet folds to make one page)
items.substitutions: Copy paper at 80 gsm works for a practice book; lighter paper (50–60 gsm) folds cleanly and makes a more traditional thin-page feel

items.name: Cover card stock, 180–200 gsm
items.qty: 2 sheets cut to the same size as the folded pages
items.substitutions: Washi kozo makes a traditional cover; any firm card at 200 gsm also works

attrs.body: Sharp blades are safer than blunt ones: blunt blades skid. Change the blade at the first sign of dulling. Always cut on a self-healing cutting mat. Always cut against a metal ruler held firmly with the non-cutting hand behind the line of cut, never in front of it. Cut away from the body.

content.text: Preparing the pages

For an A5 finished book, cut each page sheet to A4 size (210 mm × 297 mm). Fold each sheet in half, bringing the two short edges together. The fold is the foredge of the finished page; the open edges will become the spine. Press each fold crisp with the bone folder by running it along the fold under firm pressure.

Align all the folded pages in a stack with the folds at the right (foredge) and the open edges at the left (future spine). Place the front cover sheet at the top and the back cover sheet at the bottom. Tap the stack on a flat surface to align the head and tail edges. Clamp the stack firmly along the foredge or hold it under a heavy book while you mark the sewing stations.
Marking the sewing stations

Mark four sewing stations on the spine edge (the open-edges side) of the stack. All stations sit 10 mm from the spine edge. Station 1: 20 mm from the head. Station 2: 60 mm from the head. Station 3: 60 mm from the tail. Station 4: 20 mm from the tail. Adjust these positions proportionally for a different book height.

Mark each station with a light pencil dot on the front cover and use a long needle or awl tip to pierce through the stack at each mark, holding the pages very still as you work. The holes must pierce cleanly through every layer: cover, all page folds, back cover. Push the awl straight and perpendicular to the spine edge.
The yotsume sewing sequence

Thread the needle with waxed linen thread about six times the book height. Starting at station 2 (second from the head), enter from the back and bring the needle out through the front, leaving a 60 mm tail at the back.
From station 2 (front), loop around the head edge of the book and re-enter station 2 from the back.
Exit station 1 (head station) from the front.
Loop around the head edge and re-enter station 1 from the back.
Exit station 2 (front), then enter station 3 from the front.
Exit station 4 (tail station) from the back.
Loop around the tail edge and re-enter station 4 from the front.
Exit station 3 from the back. Bring the needle back up to station 2 from the back.
Tie the working thread to the tail left at station 2 with a reef knot on the back face of the cover. Trim both tails to 5 mm.

attrs.body: The yotsume pattern is symmetric: the front of the finished book shows a straight parallel band of thread from station 1 to station 4. If the thread on the front face looks like a diagonal or a cross shape, recheck the sequence: a missed head or tail loop is the most common error in this pattern.

content.text: Finishing

The finished book needs no further treatment. Press under a flat weight for an hour to flatten the spine edge. The folded foredges do not need trimming; the slight irregularity of hand-folded sheets is part of the form.

For a neater presentation, trim the head and tail of the finished stack with a craft knife against the metal ruler. Stack the book spine-down on the cutting mat, align the ruler along the head edge, and trim cleanly in a single pass through all layers. Repeat at the tail.
Care

A fukuro toji book is a stab binding: opening the pages wide will stress the spine holes over time. Hold the book with a loose grip at the spine when reading, and open only to a natural angle. The binding is durable for a light-use notebook but is not intended to be opened fully flat. Store upright on a shelf with neighbours to support both sides.
Variations

For a more decorative spine, use a contrasting thread colour. The four-station pattern shows thread on the spine face as a clear band; a second colour in the thread is visible. A split-colour thread (two colours twisted together) gives a barber-pole effect along the spine.

The same structure works in miniature: for a business-card-sized sketchbook, use 100 mm × 60 mm finished pages (cut from 100 mm × 120 mm folded sheets) with two sewing stations rather than four. A simple running stitch is enough for a two-hole version of this scale.
